Sufism (taṣawwuf) designates the mystical and spiritual dimension of Islam, oriented toward the purification of the soul (tazkiyat al-nafs) and direct experience of the Divine (kashf). Major orders (ṭuruq, sing. ṭarīqa) include the Qādiriyya, named after ʿAbd al-Qādir al-Jīlānī (d. 561 AH); the Naqshbandiyya, tracing to Bahāʾ al-Dīn Naqshband (d. 791 AH); and the Shādhiliyya, founded by Abū al-Ḥasan al-Shādhilī (d. 656 AH). Dhikr (remembrance of God) is the central practice across all ṭuruq.
